<P2B-040 Creation of Dail>
<I>
<P2B-040$A> <#> <com> In this lecture <,> I will discuss aspects of civil and military relations within the independence movement in the years immediately following Da/il E/ireann 's first meeting on the twenty-first of January nineteen nineteen </com> <#> <rep> Coincidentally but symbolically <,> this was the same day that two policemen were shot dead at Soloheadbeg in the first serious act of violence in the War of Independence </rep> <#> <rep> That day thus marked the somewhat uneasy and temporary fusion of two distinct strands in Irish nationalism <,> the constitutional and the revolutionary <,> in a unitary independence movement </rep> <#> <rep> My aims are firstly to explore key issues surrounding the control of the army <,> and secondly to comment on the consequences for the military and for defence policy in independent Ireland of what happened in the first chaotic and violent years of Da/il E/ireann 's existence </rep> <#> <rep> The defence forces have suffered ever since from the circumstances of their establishment </rep> <#> <rep> Generations of Irish politicians have heaped praise on the army as the epitome of national discipline and the guarantor of national sovereignty </rep> <#> <rep> Successive governments have nevertheless <,> failed to develop even the outlines of a credible defence policy <,> while the army has been hampered in discharging its incoherent mandate by a chronic lack of money <,> and by a Byzantine system of administration designed primarily to keep the warriors in perpetual thrall to the Department of Defence </rep> <#> <rep> The Gleeson report of nineteen ninety on pay and conditions in the defence forces <,> undoubtedly the most authoritative examination of the army yet published <,> commented+ that <,> the defence forces have traditionally enjoyed high esteem and trust <,> but their <.> in </.> internal affairs have tended to attract <,> a relatively low level of public and political interest </rep> <#> <rep> Armies and democracies tend by turn to be ignored or <,> at best <,> taken for granted <,> and then trustingly looked to on the occasion of a variety of military emergencies </rep> <#> <rep> The defence forces in this country are no exception </rep> <#> < rep> In many respects however <,> the experience of the Irish defence forces has been exceptional when measured against those of other newly-independent states </rep> <#> <rep> This is demonstrated by the remarkably low level of defence spending since independence <,> by governments ' deeply ingrained unwillingness to comment on defence policy except in those anodyne terms <,> and by the absence of any informed debate on defence issues </rep> <#> <rep> The declared primary role of the defence forces since the end of the Civil War in nineteen twenty-three <,> has been to defend the State from external attack </rep> <#> In <rep> reality <,> however <,> successive governments have relied not on the army but on the defensive umbrella provided by Britain and her allies <,> to shield the State from external aggression </rep> <#> <rep> In practice it has been the army 's dismal fate since the early nineteen twenties <,> to subsist at a size and at a level of equipment just sufficient to perpetuate the domestic illusion <,> that the State is capable of effective action against external aggression <,> and to deal with any likely internal security threat </rep> <#> <rep> The latter function has sometimes seen the army required to act <,> not so much in aid of the civil power <,> the conventional phrase used to describe its security and other activities within the State <,> but in lieu of it </rep> <#> <rep> A striking example is the use of military courts during the <.> W </.> Civil War <,> in the nineteen thirties <,> and again during the Emergency to administer justice for anti-State offences </rep> <#> <rep> The army has also had a wide miscellany of non-military jobs routinely thrust upon it </rep> <#> <rep> Some of these such as helicopter search and rescue duties <,> derive from its military functions </rep> <#> <rep> Others however <,> such as collecting rubbish during a bin strike <,> or running an emergency shelter for Dublin 's homeless <,> are given to it simply because it is a convenient source of disciplined and obedient labour </rep> <#> <rep> Independent Ireland 's defence policy in practice appears all the more curious <,> given the means by which independence was won <,> and the practical legitimacy of the new State established </rep> <#> <rep> The struggle against the military and police machines of the British government in Ireland <,> was followed by a civil war <,> in which the national army <,> defeated armed Republican resistance to the new order </rep> <#> <rep> In such circumstances <,> one might expect to find the military revered and cosseted <,> by deferential ministers anxious to keep them quiet </rep> <#> <rep> This was scarcely the army 's experience </rep> <#> <rep> Similarly <,> Nationalist Ireland 's enduring grievance about partition <,> might have been expected to influence the development and organisation of the defence forces </rep> <#> <rep> In fact <,> once the border was confirmed in December nineteen twenty-five <,> the army was required to operate as though neither the political entity nor the territory of Northern Ireland existed </rep> <#> <rep> This injunction left the defence forces desperately ill-informed <,> when the Second World War broke out </rep> <#> <rep> It caused further difficulties when the Northern Troubles flared up again in nineteen sixty-nine <,> and the possibility of Irish Army intervention was canvassed by ministers unfamiliar with modern military realities </rep> <#> <rep> Finally <,> from nineteen twenty-three the defence forces were required to plan for their nominal primary task of <.> ex </.> resisting external aggression <,> without any consultation with or explicit reference to Britain <,> which was not only the implicit strategic guarantor of Irish security but also much the most plausible threat to it <,> </rep> <#> <rep> The vicissitudes endured by the army since the early nineteen twenties <,> are explained partly by three historical factors </rep> <#> <rep> The first is the policy of military neutrality <,> adopted from the outset by the Irish Free State <,> which over the years has acquired the status of holy writ </rep> <#> <rep> It is outside the ambit of this lecture </rep> <#> <rep> The second factor is the nature of the War of Independence <,> and the third is the ambiguous and tangled relationships between and within the military and the political wings of the independence movement after nineteen thirteen </rep> <#> <rep> Between nineteen nineteen and nineteen twenty-one <,> Ireland experienced a sporadic scattered small-scale guerrilla war </rep> <#> <rep> Its object was not to inflict direct military defeat on a vastly superior enemy <,> but to make the country ungovernable <,> and to force Britain to recognise the legitimacy of Irish demands for self-rule </rep> <#> <rep> Ulster apart <,> these tactics succeeded </rep> <#> <rep> Their very success however <,> seems to have left both a generation of political leaders <,> and the public at large <,> under the impression that all that would ever be needed to deter or to expel an external aggressor was a few rifles in the right hands </rep> <#> <rep> With the exception of Richard Mulcahy and <,> arguably <,> Sean MacEntee <,> no senior politician <,> on either side of the Treaty Divide after nineteen twenty-three <,> wasted their energies on the serious consideration of external defence issues <,> until forced to do so by events </rep> <#> <rep> And despite the chastening experience of the Emergency <,> the old assumptions reasserted themselves once World War Two ended </rep> <#> <rep> What Tom Garvin has described as the extraordinary political longevity <,> of the revolutionary elite ensured that these views held currency at least until the early nineteen sixties </rep> <#> <rep> Some would say that they persist today <,> </rep> <#> <rep> The explanation for the political sensitivity of military issues <,> also lies in what happened between nineteen thirteen <,> and nineteen twenty-four </rep> <#> <rep> It is generally held that the contemporary defence forces are the direct descendants of the Irish Volunteers <,> formed in nineteen thirteen as an open Nationalist militia <,> pledged to ensure that the Irish Home Rule Bill would be applied in full </rep> <#> <rep> In June nineteen fourteen <,> the organisation was forced to accept the control of John Redmond <,> who appeared on the verge of achieving a substantial measure of independence by constitutional means <,> as long as the British government would keep its nerve on Home Rule <,> and face down the threat of violence from Unionist Ulster </rep> <#> <rep> By imposing his control on the Irish Volunteers <,> he hoped to rein in the wilder separatist elements <,> who might otherwise seek confrontation with the British authorities </rep> <#> <rep> After the outbreak of World War One <,> most volunteers took their lead from Redmond <,> and supported the British war effort </rep> <#> <rep> A minority of perhaps five percent seceded <,> retaining the title of the Irish Volunteers </rep> <#> <rep> This was a significant moment <,> because it illustrated the conditional nature of the subordination of armed to constitutional politics within the Nationalist movement </rep> <#> <rep> Two years later came the Easter Rising <,> the brainchild of the Military Council of the long-established secret society the IRB </rep> <#> <rep> It was organised behind the back of the Volunteer 's Chief of Staff Eoin MacNeill <,> by a group of his officers who ensured that the rebellion went ahead <,> despite his countermanding order </rep> <#> <rep> This illustrated the conditional nature of subordination and allegiance even within the revolutionary movement <,> and it probably contributed to the mistrust of the IRB <,> later displayed by some key political figures <,> including Eamon de Valera </rep> <#> <rep> In launching the rebellion <,> the IRB proclaimed an Irish Republic <,> declared itself the rightful government of Ireland <,> and created an Irish Republican Army <,> into which the Irish Volunteers and other combatants were automatically subsumed </rep> <#> <rep> From this it followed that the IRA <,> or the Volunteers as they continued generally to be known <,> were the military arm of the IRB 's Irish Republic </rep> <#> <rep> The IRB itself remained an oath-bound secret society <,> espousing Republican principles <,> but run on strictly hierarchical lines <,> and demanding absolute obedience from its members </rep> <#> <rep> It stood apart from the IRA and from Sinn Fe/in <,> although most IRA commanders <,> and many <.> oth </.> of the leading figures in Sinn Fe/in <,> were also IRB members </rep> <#> <rep> Florence O'Donoghue <,> a senior officer in both the IRB and the IRA <,> was later to observe that this <,> interlocking of leadership personnel <,> while it tended to unify policy and correlate activities between the different organisations within the independence movement <,> had serious disadvantages which later became apparent <,> </rep> <#> <rep> <&Irish> An </&Irish> Taoiseach Albert Reynolds recently observed <,> that amongst the first Da/il 's achievements was the establishment of the <,> primacy of the civil power over the military </rep> <#> <rep> While civilian primacy is now an unremarked cornerstone of Irish constitutional politics however <,> the evidence suggests that the question of the ultimate loyalty of the military wing of the independence movement was a vexed one <,> until the collapse of the army mutiny in nineteen twenty-four </rep> <#> <rep> Throughout the War of Independence <,> there were confusion of allegiance between the Da/il government and the IRB <,> and between the IRB and the IRA </rep> <#> <rep> After the Treaty came civil war <,> won decisively by those willing to accept the qualified independence <,> of a twenty-six county Free State <,> within the British commonwealth <,,> </rep> <#> <rep> But a rift persisted even amongst the victors </rep> <#> <rep> In March nineteen twenty-four a faction within the military <,> resentful of developments in the army since nineteen twenty-two <,> threatened the government with mutiny </rep> <#> <rep> That crisis was overcome <,> but another group of challengers to the supremacy of elected civilian government endured <,> in the Republican movement </rep> <#> <rep> Irish separatist history since nineteen thirteen is littered with such legitimist claims <,> abandoned oaths <,> and rival authorities </rep> <#> <rep> Throughout the campaign for self-determination after nineteen thirteen <,> even during what de Valera termed the <,> four years of magnificent discipline from nineteen seventeen to nineteen twenty-one <,> relations between the political and military wings of the separatist movement were fraught with uncertainty </rep> <#> <rep> That grew rather than lessened <,> after the establishment of Da/il E/ireann <,> although this did not become public knowledge until after the treaty was signed <,> </rep> <#> <rep> Sinn Fe/in 's success in the nineteen eighteen election <,> was seen as Ż+ a resounding democratic endorsement of the right to self-determination <,> to use <&Irish> An </&Irish> Taoiseach 's words once more </rep> <#> <rep> However <,> when Da/il E/ireann was established <,> it did not automatically become the only focus of allegiance and font of legitimacy <,> for members of the independence movement </rep> <#> <rep> The IRA had its own executive <,> dominated by but distinct from the IRB </rep> <#> <rep> The IRB regarded itself as the legal embodiment of the Republic proclaimed in nineteen sixteen <,> and saw the IRA as its army </rep> <#> <rep> This <,> together with its revolutionary and conspiratorial tradition <,> posed difficulties <.> f </.> for those who sought to build on the democratic authority <,> conferred by Sinn Fe/in 's electoral success and the creation of Da/il E/ireann </rep> <#> <rep> But Sinn Fe/in was itself regarded <,> less as a political party than as the civilian and subordinate wing of the independence movement <,> and there were many members of both the IRA and the IRB in it </rep>
</I>

<P2B-047$A> <#> <rep> Chancellor Vice Chancellor ladies and gentlemen uhm uh <,> I 'm really a a master of brevity </rep> <#> <rep> Uhm <,> when I uh have been asked in the past to uh speak for three quarters of an hour I 've spoken for half an hour </rep> <#> <rep> When I 've been asked to speak for twenty minutes I speak for fifteen minutes </rep> <#> <dirK> Now I 've been asked to speak for eight minutes and you can get your stopwatches out </dirK> <#> <exp> I 'm greatly honoured to be standing here participating in this ceremony with so many young and I hope <,> hopeful people </exp> <#> <rep> I use the word <mention> hopeful </mention> because it 's a word connected to the young <,> the energetic <,> those at the threshold <,> those unfatigued by the stress of living </rep> <#> <rep> Hope is the mainstay of the writer </rep> <#> <rep> I hope I will finish the novel I 'm writing </rep> <#> <rep> I hope someone will publish it </rep> <#> <rep> I hope that having got it published the critics will notice its existence </rep> <#> <rep> I hope that someone will read it </rep> <#> <rep> And then the greatest hope of all <,> I hope I will one day write another novel <,> that I will feel the stirrings and anxieties of a new creation in my head <,> very much like a woman feeling the stirrings and anxieties of new life growing in her belly </rep> <#> <rep> I get the feeling that the majority of people think that writing is easy </rep> <#> <rep> It 's well for you <,> they say <,> just sitting at home writing all day </rep> <#> <rep> They seem to think it 's like </rep> <#> <rep> The coupling of the head and the imagination can be quite fierce <,> quite painful <,> before the seed of an idea is set in the brain </rep> <#> <rep> After the birth <,> the <.> i </.> the idea which I may say gestates for months indeed sometimes painfully for years <,> has like an infant to be fed <,> and cared for <,> nurtured <,> clothed and guided through a million pitfalls until it reaches the stage you have all now reached when hopefully it is launched onto the world </rep> <#> <rep> At last it takes on its own life and the author is free to start once more to await with hope another birth </rep> <#> <rep> One of the few advantages of being a woman in the world of writing is that you recognise so well each symptom <,> each setback </rep> <#> <rep> You can perhaps <rep> That was to have been my text for this address or small sermon </rep> <#> <rep> But when I mentioned it to my daughter who is a university lecturer in Dublin her reply+ was <,> <quote> but it is </quote> </rep> <#> <rep> So that put the kibosh on that </rep> <#> <rep> In her way <,> she is right <,> but also from my point of view <,> so am I </rep> <#> <rep> What I really want to say to you you wish </rep> <#> <rep> For so many years you 've been guided through the treasure store of our literature <,> and when I say <mention> our </mention> I mean the gamut of work written in these two beating </rep> <#> <rep> To get back to the beginning <,> which is what I was always taught you should do at the end of an essay and therefore I-suppose* it should also be due at the end of an address <P2B-048$A> <#> <rep> Comrades <,,> friends <,> international guests <,> I <,> am a socialist <,> and I have news for those <,> who want to bury socialism </rep> <&> applause </&> <#> <rep> There is <rep> It 's the heart of our heartless world </rep> <#> <rep> But my intention tonight <,> is not <,> to comfort you with soothing phrases </rep> <#> <rep> It 's to challenge you to greater effort <,>
